title: Geophysical Surveying and Mapping Services

narrative.executive_view: 541360 combines physical acquisition, processing/interpretation and, in a minority of large-company cases, data licensing. AI can compress processing and reporting labor, but does not replace crews, sensor deployment, permits or accountable subsurface judgment. The target pool is only 78 firms on the frozen screen and the record therefore underwrites a thin, cyclical market rather than a repeatable roll-up. [S5]
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: SEG describes deep learning as assisting seismic interpretation and its attributes course explains that ML can reduce and classify large seismic volumes, while an interpreter still chooses inputs, labels and geological meaning. That supports material automation in processing, picking, documentation and back office; it does not support removal of field acquisition or expert QA. The frozen 4-digit occupation mix is an especially weak proxy because its residual must carry the geoscientist and crew work missing from the listed engineering-heavy roles. [S1][S2]
narrative.value_capture: The code's service mix is data collection, data sales and integrated geophysical services, but First Research says small firms compete by specialization while large firms gain scale. TGS shows that the very different multi-client-library model capitalizes acquisition and processing costs and licenses finished data non-exclusively. I therefore set only 35% savings retention and a 35% moat: a small project contractor can retain some value through local specialization and relationship, but should not be credited with the large library owner's licensing economics. [S3][S5]
narrative.adoption_timing: The direct technical evidence shows that ML/DL is a real, established seismic-interpretation workflow, not an untested concept. It does not measure adoption by US 541360 operators or effective use after field and client QA. The selected 25% current-effective-adoption and four-year t50 are consequently bounded estimates (10–40% and 3–7 years), below the frozen 50% boundary and deliberately not presented as survey observations. [S1][S2]
narrative.consolidation_economics: There is clear strategic consolidation at the top of the industry: TGS and PGS completed their merger on 1 July 2024. That documents one relevant strategic consolidator, not a lower-middle-market add-on program, so the count is one with a 0–2 documented range. No disclosed transaction supports a private $1–10M EBITDA 541360 multiple; entry at 4.0x and exit at 5.0x are explicitly bounded underwriting assumptions, not attributed to TGS, PGS, or public-company values. [S4]
narrative.terminal_demand: Paid demand for physical acquisition and technically accountable interpretation can remain, but the code is pressured rather than durable as an undifferentiated service: the software-assisted interpretation layer is becoming easier to deliver and large providers have data-library scale that a small contractor commonly lacks. TGS's library model is a useful contrast, not evidence that a small firm owns licensable data. [S3][S5]
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: This is a low-confidence aggregate. The frozen labor share combines vintages and the role mix is parent 5413 rather than six-digit geophysics; the residual drives V. Energy exploration is cyclical, the frozen n_band is concentrated in a small number of classes, and no code-specific small-firm pricing, ownership-age or transaction database was found. Deal diligence should split field-acquisition, processing, proprietary-data and energy/mining/environmental revenue before relying on any score.

sources.title: SEG: Introduction to Seismic Interpretation Using Deep Learning
sources.what_it_supports: SEG states that deep learning assists seismic-interpretation workflows and has value in common interpretation tasks; it supports technical feasibility, not a code-level adoption percentage.

sources.title: SEG: Seismic Attributes — from Interactive Interpretation to Machine Learning
sources.what_it_supports: SEG describes ML data reduction and classification in seismic interpretation and explains the continuing interpreter role in input, labels and geological interpretation.

sources.title: TGS 2024 Annual Report
sources.what_it_supports: TGS describes a multi-client library of geophysical/geological data licensed non-exclusively to oil companies, with acquisition and processing costs capitalized. It is evidence about the large data-library model, not small-firm economics or private transaction multiples.

sources.title: TGS and PGS Merger Completed
sources.what_it_supports: TGS reports that its merger with PGS formally completed on 1 July 2024; this documents one strategic consolidation event in energy geophysical data/services.

sources.title: First Research: Geophysical Surveying & Mapping Services Industry Profile
sources.what_it_supports: The profile identifies NAICS 541360 and describes the service mix as data collection, data sales and integrated geophysical services; it says small firms compete through geography or survey-type specialization while large firms gain scale.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: The frozen 4-digit mix is not representative of six-digit geophysics. The role judgments retain the prior bounded task view: ML materially affects processing/documentation, whereas field acquisition and geological accountability remain human; the residual is explicitly uncertain.
caveats: The frozen role mix is parent 5413 and its 48.44% residual contains the unlisted geoscientist, processor and field-crew roles that determine this estimate.

role_judgments.rationale: Survey-data analysis, design iterations and documentation automate more readily than accountable engineering decisions.
This parent-code occupation is likely contamination; retained fraction reflects drafting/document work only.
Scheduling, reporting and permit-package administration automate, while field coordination remains human.
Drafting and deliverable production are comparatively automatable, subject to QA.
Internal reporting and resourcing automate, but customer, safety and capital decisions do not.
Documentation and analysis automate; equipment and field-engineering decisions remain human.
Management administration automates partially; delivery accountability and client relationships persist.
Physical site inspection and evidence gathering dominate despite digital assistance.
Processing and documentation automate more than hardware and field integration.
Automation assists routine coding and pipeline maintenance, with specialized processing work retained.
Residual is a mix of processor/geoscientist work with high ML exposure and physical acquisition crews with low exposure; midpoint is deliberately broad.

pi_dist.rationale: Select 35% retained savings, below midpoint, because the code includes competitive collection and integrated project services while the higher-retention non-exclusive data-library model is a large-provider exception. Local or survey-type specialization can preserve some savings but does not establish library-style pricing.
caveats: Neither source reports small-firm AI price realization; the value is an industry-structure estimate, not an observed pass-through rate.
TGS's data-library economics are deliberately not treated as representative of a $1–10M EBITDA contractor.

pi_moat.rationale: A small operator can have geography, survey-type and client-workflow specialization, but it usually lacks TGS-scale proprietary library assets. Set a modest 35% moat rather than crediting the code with public data-major barriers.
caveats: No source measures moat or customer switching for six-digit lower-middle-market firms.
Data rights and repeat-client concentration must be tested at each target.

t50_years.rationale: ML-enabled seismic interpretation is established, but effective adoption requires data QA, workflow integration and customer acceptance. With no US 541360 adoption survey, choose a four-year midpoint within a 3–7 year range rather than infer a date from technical-course availability.
caveats: Technical evidence is not a firm-adoption census.
The selection is positive because the paired current-adoption estimate is below the frozen 50% boundary.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Set 25% for firms materially using AI in automatable functions: SEG demonstrates mature tools and workflows, while not measuring penetration among small US 541360 operators. The range remains below 50%, so this is not an implied near-complete rollout.
current_adoption_pct.plausible_range: 10%–40% of firms materially using AI in automatable functions
caveats: No code-specific current-adoption survey was found.
Use of established ML software by a geophysicist is not necessarily firm-wide effective adoption.

historical_analogs.value: Seismic interpretation has long incorporated ML-assisted reduction and classification; the analogous change is augmentation of interpreter workflow rather than replacement of field acquisition.
historical_analogs.rationale: SEG's technical descriptions support the workflow analogy and the continuing role of interpretation judgment.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Augmentation-first adoption; pace varies by data quality, client requirements and workflow maturity.
caveats: This is a qualitative analogue, not evidence of a numeric adoption curve.

selection.rationale: No 541360 owner-age census was identified. Retain a deliberately low-confidence 28% midpoint only as a neutral underwriting estimate rather than attach an unrelated professional-services demographic proxy.
caveats: No industry-specific owner-age evidence; validate seller age and succession at target level.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No dedicated six-digit succession-shortage source was identified, so the boolean remains false rather than inferring a shortage from technical labor scarcity.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: false unless code-specific evidence is obtained
caveats: A target may have a real succession need without an industry-wide documented shortage.

active_consolidators.rationale: Count one documented strategic consolidator event, TGS's completed PGS merger. Do not extrapolate the event into a broad lower-middle-market buyer count or call TGS a proven buyer of the frozen target band.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: 0–2 documented strategic consolidators relevant to energy geophysical data/services
caveats: The merger is between large public companies, not a small-firm add-on transaction.
No recurring 541360 lower-middle-market acquisition program was confirmed.

buy_mult.rationale: No disclosed transaction supports a $1–10M EBITDA 541360 entry multiple. Use 4.0x as a bounded underwriting assumption for a thin, cyclical, equipment- and project-exposed contractor, explicitly independent of public TGS/PGS values.
caveats: Not an observed industry transaction multiple.
Asset ownership, backlog, client concentration and data rights can move a target outside this range.

exit_mult.rationale: No disclosed transaction supports a private 541360 exit multiple. Set a modest one-turn uplift to 5.0x only if a buyer values diversified service mix and repeatable workflow; do not use the strategic TGS–PGS merger as a private-market comp.
caveats: Not an observed industry transaction multiple.
The uplift is contingent on target-specific backlog, data rights, field assets and customer concentration.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed data-collection, data-sale and integrated-geophysical-service revenue; smaller firms compete through geographic or survey-type specialization.
pricing_structure.rationale: This restates the observed service categories and small-firm positioning in the industry profile without claiming an unobserved standard price term or AI pass-through percentage.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Target-specific mix can range from field-project revenue to data-led or integrated services.
caveats: The source does not disclose contract terms, margins or AI-driven repricing for small firms.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: SEG supports established ML workflows, but no small-firm adoption census exists; below-50% current effective use and a positive four-year t50 consistently describe gradual implementation despite material task exposure.

terminal_value.justification: Physical acquisition and expert QA preserve paid work, but routine interpretation/processing is increasingly software-assisted and small contractors usually lack the multi-client library economics documented for TGS. [S1][S3][S5]

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V uses the frozen parent-5413 role mix only as required and discloses its mismatch. A is based only on SEG technical-workflow evidence plus a bounded adoption judgment. C uses service-mix and data-library distinctions; B counts only the documented TGS–PGS event plus un-sourced target-level caveats; M is explicitly un-sourced underwriting rather than a public-company proxy.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: The code's mix includes collection, data sales and integrated services [S5]. The 35% retained-savings estimate treats specialized delivery as providing limited retention while excluding TGS-style library economics from small contractors; it is not presented as an observed price-reset rate.

dataset_mismatch.description: The frozen role mix is parent 5413, dominated by engineering-services occupations, whereas 541360's core geoscientist, processing and acquisition-crew work is largely in the residual.
sensitivity: Changing the residual's balance between field crews and processing geoscientists materially changes V.
The frozen labor-share vintages and harmonization remain unmodified.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Energy-linked geophysical surveying, processing and interpretation
market_boundary.selection_basis: The exact-code industry profile says oil-and-gas exploration drives demand and identifies data collection, data sales and integrated geophysical services; it is used for scope framing only. [S5]

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 3.33 to 1.43 across the stated interval; no code-level adoption census anchors the midpoint.
C is highly sensitive to whether a target sells specialized field/integrated work or owns defensible data rights.
M is a bounded underwriting assumption because no private exact-code transaction comp was found.

reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates the prior Sol review: C/A/B/M selections now use relevant geophysics sources where available, each remaining estimate states a mechanism and meaningful range, and no public-major source is used as a private transaction-multiple attribution. All five score-driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21.
